moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing the Polish Opening, an unusual first move that immediately stakes a claim on the queenside by supporting a future c4 advance. While it looks offbeat, b4 controls important central squares (c5 and a5) and prepares to fianchetto our bishop on b2, where it will aim at the long diagonal toward g7. This move lets us play against Black's center before committing our own pieces, which is the essence of the Polish style—flexible, positional, and forcing Black to reveal their intentions first.

Black seizes the center aggressively, which is a natural response to our queen's-side maneuvering. By playing e5, Black controls d4 and f4, prevents us from easily pushing c4 (our thematic follow-up), and gains space in the middle. This is a solid, principled reply that challenges us to prove our opening has enough compensation for the unorthodox move order.

Now we complete our fianchetto setup, planting the bishop on the long diagonal where it eyes g7 and controls the important dark squares running toward Black's kingside. Bb2 is flexible—it supports both our queen-side expansion with c4 and reinforces the center, while keeping our position coherent. The bishop on b2 is also a characteristic feature of the Polish Opening, giving us long-range pressure and often supporting our pieces in the center.

Black immediately attacks our queen-side foothold and locks the pawn structure there, preventing our planned c4 break. By playing c5, Black gains space on the queenside and makes it clear they're fighting for central control rather than allowing us to dictate the game. This move shows Black understands the Polish Opening's slow, maneuvering nature—they're forcing us to find concrete plans rather than accept a cramped position.
